perturbation.original_text: THIS MAINTENANCE AGREEMENT IS GOVERNED BY, AND SHALL BE SUBJECT TO, THE TERMS AND CONDITIONS OF THE MASTER AGREEMENT BETWEEN NETZEE AND BANKERS BANK, INCLUDING THE LIMITED WARRANTY AND DISCLAIMER AND LIMITATIONS OF LIABILITY PROVIDED THEREIN. SUCH TERMS AND CONDITIONS ARE HEREBY AFFIRMED BY BOTH PARTIES AND INCORPORATED BY REFERENCE IN THIS MAINTENANCE AGREEMENT, INCLUDING, WITHOUT LIMITATION, ALL LICENSES GRANTED OR EXERCISABLE THEREUNDER.
perturbation.changed_text: SUCH TERMS AND CONDITIONS ARE HEREBY AFFIRMED BY BOTH PARTIES AND INCORPORATED BY REFERENCE IN THIS MAINTENANCE AGREEMENT, INCLUDING, WITHOUT LIMITATION, ALL LICENSES GRANTED OR EXERCISABLE THEREUNDER. THIS MAINTENANCE AGREEMENT IS GOVERNED BY, AND SHALL BE SUBJECT TO, THE TERMS AND CONDITIONS OF THE MASTER AGREEMENT BETWEEN NETZEE AND BANKERS BANK.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the disclaimer of warranty and limitations of liability to the end of the sentence buries important information within less critical details, obscuring it from quick recognition and comprehension. Making it harder for Bankers Bank to know about the disclaimer and limitation of liability.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-316 requires that disclaimers of warranty be conspicuous. By burying the warranty disclaimer at the end of the paragraph, it is less likely to be considered conspicuous and thus may violate the UCC.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this Article on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2-202 ) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2)Subject to subsection (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that "There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof." (3)Notwithstanding subsection (2) (a) unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like "as is", "with all faults" or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty; and (b) when the buyer before entering into the contract has examined the goods or the sample or model as fully as he desired or has refused to examine the goods there is no implied warranty with regard to defects which an examination ought in the circumstances to have revealed to him; and (c) an implied warranty can also be excluded or modified by course of dealing or course of performance or usage of trade. (4) Remedies for breach of warranty can be limited in accordance with the provisions of this Article on liquidation or limitation of damages and on contractual modification of remed
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts UCC § 2-316 regarding the conspicuousness of warranty disclaimers. By moving the disclaimer to the end of the sentence and embedding it within less important details, the modification reduces its prominence. As the Law Explanation states, this placement makes it less likely to be considered conspicuous, thereby potentially violating UCC § 2-316. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states that to exclude or modify implied warranties, the language 'must be conspicuous'. The modification undermines this requirement by obscuring the disclaimer.

perturbation.original_text: Following the completion of such maintenance term, Bankers Bank may, at its option, renew maintenance for subsequent periods of one (1) year each, subject to adjustments proposed by Netzee not to exceed 5% at least sixty (60) days in advance of the applicable renewal date.
perturbation.changed_text: Following the completion of such maintenance term, Bankers Bank may, at its option, renew maintenance for subsequent periods of one (1) year each.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause about the 5% maximum increase and 60-day notice allows Netzee to increase maintenance prices without limit and with no advance notice. This creates uncertainty and potential financial risk for Bankers Bank and may violate standards of good faith and fair dealing in contract law.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 1-304 states that every contract or duty within the UCC imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Removing the limit on price increases and advance notice could be seen as a violation of good faith, as it allows Netzee to take unfair advantage of Bankers Bank during renewal.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes a specific protection for Bankers Bank, allowing Netzee to increase maintenance prices without limit or advance notice. This contradicts the UCC § 1-304's requirement of good faith in contract performance, as the changed text could allow Netzee to unfairly exploit Bankers Bank during renewal. The scraped law snippet 1 explicitly states that 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' By removing the limits on price increases and advance notice, the modified contract creates a potential scenario where Netzee could act in bad faith, thus contradicting the UCC.

perturbation.original_text: Maintenance provided by Netzee during the term of this Agreement shall include (1) standard maintenance upgrades and modifications offered by Netzee to its customers generally, standard maintenance upgrades and modifications offered by Netzee to its customers generally, and for such purpose Netzee will use commercially reasonable efforts to make and include (without limitation) as such modifications changes and upgrades necessary to comply with generally applicable industry and regulatory requirements of which it is notified (2) 5x12 call-in support between hours of 6:30 a.m. and 6:30 p.m. Eastern Time Monday through Friday, (3) in the event of material discrepancies between the Services as provided and the specifications for such Services (which shall be furnished in reasonably acceptable form), reasonable effort (in relation to importance, impact, and scheduled development plans) to correct those discrepancies, and (4) availability of basic Services with at least 99% uptime between 4:00 a.m Monday and 7:00 p.m. Friday and between 8:00 a.m. Saturday and 2:00 p.m. Saturday Eastern Time, exclusive of outages caused by off-peak prescheduled maintenance or causes beyond Netzee's reasonable control (including third-party equipment or communications).
perturbation.changed_text: Maintenance provided by Netzee during the term of this Agreement shall include (1) standard maintenance upgrades and modifications offered by Netzee to its customers generally, standard maintenance upgrades and modifications offered by Netzee to its customers generally, (2) 5x12 call-in support between hours of 6:30 a.m. and 6:30 p.m. Eastern Time Monday through Friday, (3) in the event of material discrepancies between the Services as provided and the specifications for such Services (which shall be furnished in reasonably acceptable form), reasonable effort (in relation to importance, impact, and scheduled development plans) to correct those discrepancies, and (4) availability of basic Services with at least 99% uptime between 4:00 a.m Monday and 7:00 p.m. Friday and between 8:00 a.m. Saturday and 2:00 p.m. Saturday Eastern Time, exclusive of outages caused by off-peak prescheduled maintenance or causes beyond Netzee's reasonable control (including third-party equipment or communications).
perturbation.explanation: The changes and upgrades necessary to comply with generally applicable industry and regulatory requirements have been removed. This could potentially lead to the software not complying with regulatory requirements. By not taking necessary actions, the business may be in breach of regulatory requirements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Deposit Insurance Act, 12 U.S. Code § 1818
perturbation.law_explanation: 12 U.S.C. § 1818 grants federal banking agencies the power to issue cease and desist orders against institutions engaging in unsafe or unsound practices or violating laws or regulations. If Netzee fails to provide necessary maintenance upgrades to comply with banking regulations, potentially placing Bankers Bank in non-compliance, this could constitute an 'unsafe or unsound practice' subject to regulatory action under this statute.

perturbation.justification: The original contract ensures compliance with industry and regulatory requirements through necessary maintenance upgrades. The changed text removes this provision, potentially leading to non-compliance. The Law Explanation points to 12 U.S.C. § 1818, which allows federal banking agencies to issue cease and desist orders for 'unsafe or unsound practices' or violations. If the removal of the compliance clause places the bank in non-compliance, this contradicts the statute. While the scraped snippets do not directly define 'unsafe or unsound practices,' they highlight the existence of 12 U.S.C. § 1818 and related enforcement actions, supporting the idea that failure to maintain regulatory compliance can lead to legal repercussions. The removal creates a structural flaw leading to a legal contradiction.
